text: Mechanical loading of tendon tissue results in upregulation of collagen expression and increased synthesis of collagen protein, regulated by the strain experienced by resident tenocytes.
Collagen formation in tendons peaks around 24 hours after exercise and remains elevated for approximately 3 days.
Collagen degradation in tendons also rises after exercise but peaks earlier than collagen synthesis, creating a net catabolic window before net anabolic adaptation occurs.
Tendinopathy is characterized by pain during activity, localized tenderness upon palpation, swelling, and impaired performance.

Histological changes in tendinopathic tendons include reduced and rounded fibroblasts, increased proteoglycans/glycosaminoglycans/water content, hypervascularization, and disorganized collagen fibrils.
Tendon microrupture and material fatigue from repetitive loading are proposed injury mechanisms for tendinopathy, implying structural weak links exist within the tendon.
Allowing at least 24-72 hours between high tendon-loading sessions supports the collagen synthesis cycle and may reduce tendinopathy risk.
